def: The basic unit of life — all living things are made of cells
A cell with NO nucleus and NO membrane-bound organelles. Example: bacteria.
A cell WITH a nucleus and WITH membrane-bound organelles. Example: plant and animal cells.
A small structure inside a cell that has a specific job — means "little organ"
The control center of the cell — holds DNA and sends instructions
The molecule that carries genetic instructions for life — shaped like a twisted ladder (double helix)
DNA in its spread-out, tangled form — like a messy pile of yarn
DNA coiled up tightly into a compact X or rod shape — happens when a cell is ready to divide
A tiny machine that builds proteins — made in the nucleolus
The flexible outer boundary of ALL cells — controls what enters and leaves
The jelly-like fluid filling the cell — everything between the membrane and the nucleus
The network of organelles that builds, processes, and ships proteins — includes ER, Golgi, and vesicles
Network of folded membranes covered in ribosomes — folds and transports proteins (the "highway")
Stacked membranes that modify, package, and sort proteins for delivery — the "packaging center"
A small membrane bubble that carries materials between organelles
Organelle that breaks down waste, damaged parts, and foreign invaders — the "garbage collector"
Organelle that converts glucose into ATP energy — the "engine" or "powerhouse" of the cell
Organelle that captures sunlight to make glucose through photosynthesis — only in plant cells
The energy molecule cells use to do work — like a rechargeable battery
The green pigment inside chloroplasts that absorbs sunlight energy
A thick, rigid outer layer made of cellulose — gives plant cells their square shape
One very large water storage compartment in plant cells — keeps the plant firm when full
The pressure of water inside the central vacuole pushing against the cell wall — keeps plants firm
The material that makes plant cell walls rigid — gives plants structural support
Small structures in animal cells that help organize cell division
The process by which plants use sunlight to convert CO₂ and water into glucose and oxygen
The process by which cells break down glucose to release ATP energy — happens in mitochondria
A small, dense area inside the nucleus where ribosomes are made
The double membrane surrounding the nucleus — has pores that let messages in and out
A lipid with a hydrophilic phosphate head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails — the building block of cell membranes
Two layers of phospholipids arranged tail-to-tail — the basic structure of all cell membranes
Water-loving — dissolves in or is attracted to water (polar or charged molecules)
Water-fearing — does NOT dissolve in water (nonpolar molecules like oils and fats)
Having both a water-loving (hydrophilic) part and a water-fearing (hydrophobic) part in the same molecule
Allows some materials through but not others — 'semi' means partial
The scientific model of the cell membrane — 'fluid' because parts move freely, 'mosaic' because it has many different pieces
A molecule that sits between phospholipids and stabilizes the membrane — keeps it not too stiff and not too loose
A protein permanently embedded in the membrane — often goes all the way through both layers
A protein that sits on the surface of the membrane — only temporarily attached, does not go through the bilayer
A protein with carbohydrate (sugar) chains attached — acts as an ID tag on the cell surface
A lipid with carbohydrate (sugar) chains attached — part of the cell's identity coating on the outer surface
The sugar-coated outer surface of a cell made of glycoproteins and glycolipids — like an ID card that the immune system reads
A membrane protein that forms a tunnel (pore) for specific ions or small molecules to pass through
A membrane protein that grabs a molecule, changes shape, and releases it on the other side of the membrane
A membrane protein that receives chemical signals from outside the cell and triggers a response inside
